coredata.dc:title: Multidimensional graphene and ZnO-based heterostructure for flexible transparent ultraviolet photodetector
coredata.dc:creator: Ko, Kang Bok
Ryu, Beo Deul
Han, Min
Hong, Chang-Hee
Dinh, Duc Anh
Cuong, Tran Viet
coredata.dc:description: A multidimensional graphene and ZnO-based heterostructure for flexible transparent ultraviolet photodetector (UV-PD) applications is successfully fabricated on an indium tin oxide-coated polyethylene terephthalate substrate (ITO-coated PET). In this prototype, chemically reduced graphene oxide film is used as a charge transfer network and a template layer for the low-temperature growth of high quality vertically aligned zinc oxide nanorods array (ZNRA) on ITO-coated PET substrate. Graphene quantum dots decorated on the ZNRA significantly enhanced the photocurrent of the device. The synergistic effects of the rGO film and GQDs on the performance of the UV-PD are also systematically explored. The fabricated flexible UV-PD shows high detectivity (~1012 cmHz1/2 W−1), high responsivity (~13 AW−1) and good response time (~5 s) at a low bias of 2 V. Importantly, the fabrication process is facile, inexpensive, solution-based, low-temperature (120 °C), and provides a promising platform for flexible transparent UV-PD applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Zinc oxide
Quantum dots
Flexible substrate
Ultraviolet photodetectors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the ITO bottom electrode

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supposed by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF-2017R1D1A3B03030758 ) and ( NRF-2016R1D1A1A09919950 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the rGO/ZNRA/GQDs UV-PD on ITO-coated PET. (b) Energy band diagram of the materials used in the UV-PD and fabricated device under test.

(a) Transmittance spectra of the rGO, rGO/ZNRA, and rGO/ZNRA/GQDs films on the ITO-coated PET, inset: the image of the fabricated device. (b) PL spectra of the rGO/GQDs, rGO/ZNRA, and rGO/ZNRA/GQDs films. (c) XPS spectra of the rGO and rGO/ZNRA films.
(a, b, c) Cross-sectional SEM image and (d, e, f) top down SEM images of the rGO1, rGO3, and rGO5 samples, respectively.

(a) I-V characteristics of the rGO3 and rGO5 samples under illumination (365 nm UV LED, 100 μWcm−2) and in the dark. (b) Time-response of the rGO3 and rGO5 samples at a bias of 2 V, and under illumination (365 nm UV LED, 100 μWcm−2). (c) Photocurrent of the rGO3 sample at a bias of 2 V as a response to different illumination powers of the 365 nm UV LED.
Energy dispersive X-ray spectrum recorded in the marked point from the inset TEM image of GQDs spray-coated on ZNRA.

(a) Time-response of the GQD0, GQD1, GQD2, and GQD3 samples at a bias of 2 V, and under illumination (365 nm UV LED, 100 μWcm−2). (b) Magnified graph of the time-response of the GQD0 and GQD1 samples. (c) Responsivity curve of the GQD0 and GQD2 samples.
Comparison of the rise time, fall time, dark current density, photocurrent density and photosensitivity of the rGO3, rGO5, GQD0, GQD1, GQD2, and GQD3 samples.

head.ce:title: Multidimensional graphene and ZnO-based heterostructure for flexible transparent ultraviolet photodetector

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Semiconductor Physics Research Center, School of Semiconductor and Chemical Engineering, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju 561-756, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Semiconductor and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: LED Agri-Bio Fusion Technology Research Center, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju 561-853, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: LED Agri-Bio Fusion Technology Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NTT Hi-Tech Institute, Nguyen Tat Thanh University, 298-300 A Nguyen Tat Thanh Street, Ho Chi Minh City, Viet Nam

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: LED Agri-Bio Fusion Technology Research Center, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju 561-853, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: LED Agri-Bio Fusion Technology Research Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multidimensional graphene and ZnO-based heterostructure for flexible transparent ultraviolet photodetector (UV-PD) applications is successfully fabricated on an indium tin oxide-coated polyethylene terephthalate substrate (ITO-coated PET). In this prototype, chemically reduced graphene oxide film is used as a charge transfer network and a template layer for the low-temperature growth of high quality vertically aligned zinc oxide nanorods array (ZNRA) on ITO-coated PET substrate. Graphene quantum dots decorated on the ZNRA significantly enhanced the photocurrent of the device. The synergistic effects of the rGO film and GQDs on the performance of the UV-PD are also systematically explored. The fabricated flexible UV-PD shows high detectivity (~1012 cmHz1/2 W−1), high responsivity (~13 AW−1) and good response time (~5 s) at a low bias of 2 V. Importantly, the fabrication process is facile, inexpensive, solution-based, low-temperature (120 °C), and provides a promising platform for flexible transparent UV-PD applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet photodetectors (UV-PDs) have great potential in many civilian and military applications, such as environmental monitoring, large area displays, and optical communication. Next generation UV-PDs, which are fabricated on bendable soft substrates instead of conventional rigid ones, have attracted a lot of attention because they can be integrated with curved cell phones, flexible displays, and other smart, wearable and foldable electronic devices. However, the fabrication of UV-PDs, which satisfy both flexibility and transparency requirements, still faces many challenges. The selected active materials must be compatible with low processing temperature and high surface roughness of common flexible substrates. On the other hand, the important criteria of device performance, including high responsivity in the UV region, high transmittance in the visible region, fast response time, visible light rejection (visible-blind), and high stability under bending stress must be maintained.

Among the variety of materials, ZnO, which is environment-friendly, suitable for low-temperature process and intrinsically visibly-blind, is emerging as a promising material for next-generation flexible transparent UV-PDs [1]. Different nanostructures of ZnO have been employed in UV-PDs, such as ZnO nanowire networks [2,3], ZnO nanocrystals [4], ZnO tetrapods [5], and ZnO nanorods [6]. In particular, vertically aligned ZnO nanorods arrays (ZNRA) have attracted a lot of attention due to their high internal photoconductive gain and advantages in light scattering and absorption. ZNRA can be fabricated by inexpensive and simple growth techniques such as electrodeposition and hydrothermal growth on a variety of flexible substrates, such as paper [6], polyethylene terephthalate (PET) [7,8], and polyimide (PI) substrates [9]. Moreover, the responsivity of ZNRA-based UV-PDs can be improved by incorporating Au nanoparticles (NPs) [10], Ag NPs [11], CuO NPs [12], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [13,14], and carbon quantum dots [15].

A further challenge of the ZNRA-based flexible UV-PDs is the slow response time (up to a few hundred seconds), because of the high surface defect density of ZnO, which leads to slow O2 adsorption and desorption processes [13,16]. ITO-coated PET, dominantly used by the industry for flexible electronics, has a very low processing temperature (120 °C) and high surface roughness, which inhibits the growth of high quality ZNRA [17]. To overcome these challenges, we report herein the use of a chemically reduced graphene oxide (rGO) template layer in a multidimensional rGO/ZNRA/GQDs flexible UV-PD. The rGO film acts not only as an underlying charge transfer network but also a template layer to enable uniform coating of the ZnO seed layer on the ITO surface, and the decorated GQDs act as photosensitizers. The synergistic effects of the ZNRA, rGO film, and GQDs on the optoelectronic performance of our flexible UV-PD were systematically investigated. Our device demonstrated a transmittance of around 70% in the spectral range of 400–700 nm, high detectivity (~1012 cmHz1/2 W−1), high responsivity (~13 AW−1) as well as good response time (~10 s) at a low bias of 2 V. Importantly, the fabrication process is facile, inexpensive, solution-based, low-temperature, and offers a promising platform for future flexible transparent UV-PDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ITO bottom electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The purchased ITO-coated PET substrates (I-one Film, South Korea) were first immersed and sonicated in a mixture of acetone, ethanol, and DI water of a ratio of 1:1:1 for 5 min, and blow-dried with nitrogen before being loaded into an ozone chamber for 30 min under intense UV light. These processes remove organic contaminations and simultaneously gave the ITO surface a negative charge by oxygenated functionalization. Then we produced the ITO bottom electrodes by a photolithography process using a shadow mask, followed by chemical wet-etching by hydrochloric acid (HCl 36% for 1 min).

The synthesis of the chemically reduced rGO film was described in detail elsewhere [18]. In this work, we used an excess amount of hydrazine reducing agent and a longer reduction time. The rGO dispersion was then spray-coated on a prepared PET substrate preheated at 120 °C.

A gallium-doped ZnO (GZO) seed solution was synthesized as described in detail elsewhere [19]. Then we spin coated the GZO seed solution onto the rGO film and annealed at 120 °C for 1 h. After forming the seed layer, the ZNRA was grown by using a conventional hydrothermal growth process [19].

The high PL quantum yield N-doped GQDs were synthesized using citric acid (CA) and ethylene diamine (EDA) as precursors, as described in detail elsewhere [20]. The GQDs dispersion was then spray-coated on top of the ZNRA.

Photoluminescence (PL) measurement was performed using a SpectraPro-300i monochromator (Acton). The samples were excited using a 325 nm HeCd laser with an output of 10 mW power at room temperature. The optical properties of the sample were also investigated by UV–Vis measurement systems (Hewlett Packard 8453). The morphological and elemental investigations have been performed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4200) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The current-voltage (I-V) characteristics were measured under the exposure from a 365 nm UV light emitting diode (LED) using a semiconductor characterization system (Keithley 4200-MS Tech). The responsivity spectrum was measured by a Xenon lamp (450 W) and by an Oriel Cornerstone 130 1/8 m monochromator as a light source.

Fig. 1a shows the schematic diagram of our rGO/ZNRA/GQDs flexible transparent UV-PD on an ITO-coated PET substrate. The active area is 1 × 1 cm2, and the distance between the electrode fingers is 1 mm. Fig. 1b shows the energy band diagram of the different materials in our device and the fabricated device under testing shown in the inset. The proper energy level alignment of the rGO/ZNRA/GQDs heterostructure is important to the detection of UV signal [21–23]. ZnO has a band gap of around 3.3 eV with the conduction band at 4.2–4.4 eV below vacuum level [13]. GQDs have a band gap of around 1.5 eV with the conduction band at 1.4–3.0 eV and valence band at 4.3–5.4 eV below vacuum level [24]. Under UV illumination, electron−hole pairs are generated in both ZNRA and GQDs. The photo-generated electrons can transfer from GQDs to the ZNRA because the conduction band of GQDs is higher than that of ZnO, meanwhile, the holes migrate to the surface of ZNRA due to the band bending and react with negatively charged oxygen ions to begin the desorption process [h+ + O2 −(ad) → O2(g)]. The unpaired electrons enhance the free carrier concentration in the heterostructures and increase the photocurrent. Fig. 2a shows the transmittance spectra of the rGO, rGO/ZNRA and rGO/ZNRA/GQDs films on ITO-coated PET substrate. For the rGO/ZNRA and rGO/ZNRA/GQDs films, the transmittance is higher than 70% in the visible range (400–700 nm). Similarly, it has been reported by Son et al. that the transmittance of the ZnO quantum dots/graphene structure on PET substrate is around 80% [25]. The inset of Fig. 2a shows our device in front of a background image, which demonstrates its high optical transparency. The four black dots are drawn on the backside of the PET substrate to indicate the positions of the bottom ITO electrodes (Fig. 1a).

Fig. 2b shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the rGO/GQDs, rGO/ZNRA and rGO/ZNRA/GQDs films. ZNRA grown by a conventional hydrothermal process usually consists of many lattice defects caused by excess Zn atoms and oxygen vacancies, which leads to a high intensity defect emission. Annealing at high temperature is often necessary to produce high quality ZNRA, which hinders its growth on flexible substrates. Han et al. reported a suppression of the defect emission by capping the as-grown ZNRA with a thermally reduced rGO film [26]. The main features of the PL spectrum of our rGO/ZNRA film are the near band-edge UV emission peak at 390 nm and a broad visible defect related emission peak at 533 nm, with the intensity ratio between the UV and defect emissions being more than two. It has been understood from our previous work that the ZNRA grown on a GO film has reduced oxygen vacancies and a higher intensity ratio between the UV and defect emissions. It was proposed to be associated with oxygen related functional groups attached on the GO surface. These functional groups enable for not only improving adhesion of the ZnO seed during the spin coating process but also transferring oxygen atoms to ZnO nanorods during the hydrothermal growth process [27]. The PL spectrum of the rGO/GQDs film shows a broad peak at 450 nm, which is consistent with previous reports [20,28]. Compared to the rGO/ZNRA film, the PL spectrum of the rGO/ZNRA/GQDs film show a red shift, broader width, and slightly enhanced UV emission, which has not yet been reported in previous researches on ZNRA decorated with GQDs [13,14,29]. A possible mechanism to interpret red-shifted phenomenon is that the coupling between GQDs and ZNRA induced splitting of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of the GQDs into orbitals with distinct lower energy levels wherein enables electron transitions to the ZNRA ground state, and resulted in red-shifted in the PL spectrum. This observation reveals that there is an interaction between GQDs and ZNRA. Fig. 2c shows the XPS spectra of the rGO and rGO/ZNRA films. The values of the binding energy of Zn2p3/2, Zn2p1/2 and O1s core-levels of the rGO/ZNRA film are 1022 eV, 1045 eV, and 531 eV, respectively, which are characteristic of ZnO [30].

Well-aligned and high aspect ratio ZNRA is crucial to the performance of the ZNRA-based UV-PDs, as well as photocatalytic, electronic and photovoltaic device applications [31]. Ikizler et al. reported in details about the relation between the morphologies of the ZNRA and the seed layer production methods. The morphologies of the ZNRA could be tuned by changing the growth time, solution composition, or seed layer density [32]. However, limited work exists on the relation between the thin films under the seed layer and the characteristics of the ZNRA evolving from these thin films [33,34]. In the first part of our experiment, we fabricated three rGO/ZNRA UV-PDs, which were spray-coated with 1 ml, 3 ml, and 5 ml of the rGO dispersion, and denoted by rGO1, rGO3, and rGO5, respectively. Fig. 3 shows the cross-sectional and top-down SEM images of the rGO1, rGO3, and rGO5 samples. The average diameters of the ZnO nanorods in the rGO1, rGO3 and rGO5 sample are 55 nm, 102 nm, and 185 nm, respectively. The average lengths are 0.45 μm, 0.74 μm, and 0.78 μm, respectively, and the average aspect ratios are 8.18, 7.25, and 4.21, respectively. It should be noted that grown ZNRA herein are shorter and thicker than that of our previous work even ZnO nanorods were formed on identical GO template layer [27]. It was attributed to the low annealed temperature of ZnO seeded layer (120 °C) since the lower annealing temperature results in the larger ZnO seed size. Moreover, our results show that thicker rGO template results in the larger diameter of the ZnO nanorods. We suggest a possible explanation as follows. ZNRA hydrothermal growth can be divided into two stages: nucleation and growth. Zhou et al. reported that high OH− concentration in the growth solution promote nucleation [32]. More spray-coated rGO provides more oxygen functional groups, which results in an accelerated nucleation and larger diameters of the ZnO nanorods. It can also be observed that the ZNRA in the rGO3 sample has the best vertical alignment. This is attributed to the following reasons: (i) the rGO5 sample has a high overlap between the rGO nanosheets, which results in clusters of the ZnO nanorods at the edge of the sheets; (ii) the rGO1 sample has mostly disjointed rGO nanosheets, and thus, the orientation of the ZnO nanorods is highly irregular.

Fig. 4a shows the I-V characteristics of the rGO3 and rGO5 UV-PDs under illumination by a 365 nm UV LED (100 μWcm−2) and in the dark. The rGO1 sample, which is not shown, does not exhibit Ohmic behavior, probably because the amount of spray-coated rGO is insufficient to provide a connection between the electrodes. The rGO3 and rGO5 samples show good Ohmic behavior and photoconductive gains under illumination. The rGO5 sample has a higher dark current than the rGO3 sample because it has a thicker rGO film and lower sheet resistance. Fig. 4b shows the time-response of the rGO3 and rGO5 sample at a bias of 2 V, under illumination by a 365 nm UV LED. It is clear to see that the dynamic response of rGO5 is unsaturated due to persistent photoconductivity phenomenon. However, the time cycle (on/off) for light irradiation was maintained to analyze the response time between rGO5 and rGO3 samples. Table 1 shows the rise times, fall times, dark current densities, photocurrent densities, photosensitivities, defined as S = (Jlight − Jdark)/Jdark, responsivities, and detectivities of the rGO3 and rGO5 samples. The rise time is measured as the time required for the current of the photodetector rising from 10% to 90% of its saturation level under illumination, and inversely for the fall time. The detectivity can be obtained by the following relation:
D λ
=
R λ
2 q
J dark
where q is the electron charge, the illumination wavelength λ is 365 nm, D
λ is measured in cmHz1/2 W−1 (or Jones), R
λ is the responsivity in AW−1, J
dark is the dark current density in A cm−2 [14].

The reduced carrier transit times in the thin ZnO nanorods contributed to the faster rise and fall times of the rGO3 sample. The higher photoconduction gain can be attributed to the higher aspect ratio and better vertical alignment. Leelavathi et al. suggests that the high aspect ratio ZNRA has a higher photoconduction gain due to a higher amount of chemisorbed oxygen species on the surface [35]. Better vertical aligned ZnO nanorods leads to an increase in the optical path length of the incident light in the active layer and an increase in light absorption efficiency [36]. Fig. 4c shows the time-response of the rGO3 sample at a bias of 2 V, under different illumination power of the 365 nm UV LED, which is obeyed to Rose's theory. It revealed that the response time is longer at the lower light intensity (25 μW/cm2). In other words, the response time is shorter at higher light intensity. As light intensity increases, quasi-Fermi levels of electrons and holes formed at the time of light irradiation will move toward the conduction and valence bands. Carrier trap centers will progressively be converted to recombination centers, and will eventually increase the response speed [37]. Our results have shown that the rGO template layer has significant impacts on the morphology of the ZNRA, and the dark current characteristic and the temporal response of the rGO/ZNRA UV-PD. Therefore, optimal rGO template layer is crucial to improve the performance of rGO/ZNRA UV-PD on ITO-coated PET film.

The second part of our experiment investigated the effects of decorated GQDs on our rGO/ZNRA UV-PDs structure. GQDs are viable in flexible transparent UV-PD applications but the response time of such UV-PDs can be as high as a few hundred seconds [13,14,24]. We deposited 0 ml, 1 ml, 2 ml, and 3 ml of the GQDs dispersion on four identical rGO3 samples, and denoted the new samples by GQD0, GQD1, GQD2, and GQD3, respectively. The energy dispersive X-ray spectrum recorded in the marked point from the inset STEM image of an individual ZNRA decorated with 2 ml of the GQDs (GQD2) is exhibited in Fig. 5 . The presence of the Zn, the O, and the C peaks were clearly observed in the spectrum. It implied that GQDs were successfully decorated on surface of ZNRA. However, the insert STEM image indicated that GQDs were accumulated and randomly distributed on the surface of ZNRA. This observation is likely due to spray-coating characteristic of GQDs dispersion carried out at low temperature.

Fig. 6a shows the time-response of the GQD0, GQD1, GQD2, and GQD3 samples at a bias of 2 V, under illumination by the 365 nm UV LED (100 μW cm−2). For clarity, Fig. 6b shows the time response of the GQD0 and GQD1 samples only. Fig. 6c shows the responsivity curves of the GQD0 and GQD2 samples. Table 1 shows the rise times, fall times, dark current densities, photocurrent densities, photosensitivities, responsivities and detectivities of the GQD0, GQD1, GQD2 and GQD3 samples. We can observe that more spray-coated GQDs results in a higher dark current, a higher photocurrent and a higher response time of the device, which can be explained as follows. First, the decorated n-type N-doped GQDs, synthesized fromcitric acid and ethylene diamine [20], increase the free carrier concentration of the n-type ZnO nanorods array, thus increase the dark current. Second, under UV light illumination, electron-hole pairs are generated in both GQDs and ZnO. However, this process is limited by the photodesorption rate of adsorbed oxygen on the surface of ZnO [29]. At a low level of decorated GQDs, the photodesorption rate changed very little. However, as more GQDs are deposited, the surface area of ZnO nanorods is reduced significantly, which slows down the desorption rate of oxygen. As a result, compared to the pristine GQD0 sample, the GQD1 and GQD2 sample achieved a significant increase in photocurrent and an eligible degrading of the response time. On the other hand, GQD3 achieved two orders of magnitude higher photocurrent but suffered from very high response time, which is typical of GQDs and ZnO heterostructure [14,38]. Our results have shown the important effects of the decorated GQDs on the dark current, the photocurrent and the response time of the rGO/ZNRA/GQDs UV-PD. There have been many attempts at ZnO-based flexible and transparent UV-PDs. However, compared to these previous reports such as ZnO on Cu nanowires [39], ZnO-graphene hybrids [40], and ZnO/Au nanoparticle [41], our device structure has achieved the simple and cheap fabrication at the lower temperature. In fact, designed device platform exhibits significantly longer response time and lower photosensitivity as compared to the self-powered photodetectors based on CdSe QDs by means of the low-temperature process [42]. However, the key components of our device structure are the ZNRA grown on the ITO electrode on PET using chemically reduced GO charge transfer network which have a great potential in the application of transparent and flexible devices. Moreover, the decorated GQDs offered an additional boost in performance. We have also shown that careful optimization of the different components is required to maintain high performance of our device structure.

In conclusion, we have developed a multidimensional heterostructure UV-PD consisting of a 2-D rGO film, a 1-D ZNRA, and 0-D GQDs. The chemically reduced GO template layer enables uniform coating of the ZnO seed and reducing oxygen vacancy in ZNRA, leads to higher quality ZNRA grown on common ITO-coated PET substrate. The effects of the rGO template layer on the morphology of the ZNRA and the performance of the device were explored. The decorated GQDs were found to simultaneously increase the photocurrents and degrade the response time of the device. As a result, we believe that the rGO/ZNRA/GQDs heterostructure platform is a good candidate for future flexible transparent UV-PD application, because of the synergy between ZnO and graphene-based materials both of which are suitable for low-temperature processes and offer high performance.

This research was supposed by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2017R1D1A3B03030758) and (NRF-2016R1D1A1A09919950).
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
